verdict.reason:
Verified against the paper. §2 Related Work carries three claims that the paper's own later sections explicitly retract, and §1 was already rewritten to the corrected framing — so §2 internally contradicts §1/§6.2/§6.5/§7.1.

(a) §2 line 136-138: "Our contribution to this thread is the *mode* account: which conditioning content a given model can bank is governed by a measurable trait (§6.2)". FALSE per the paper. §6.2 (line 316-319) says imprintability predicts only the *magnitude* of banking, explicitly "the *kind* of banking... is resolved in §6.5". §6.5 (line 437-448) states "no clean two-way semantic/surface mode" and that magnitude and kind "are not the same trait"; the very word "*mode*" is retracted ("We retract the stronger 'Gemma banks meaning' reading", line 448). So the trait does NOT govern *which content* is banked.

(b) §2 line 138: "the right task-aware prime is *model-specific* — a prescription these retention-based methods do not provide," phrased as flowing from the trait ("governed by a measurable trait... so the right task-aware prime is model-specific"). This is the trait-indexed prescription §7.1 (line 533-538) explicitly **withdraws**: conditioning value "barely correlates with imprintability (r=0.29)... we therefore **withdraw** the earlier 'decision rule indexed by imprinting character'." §1 (line 90-92) already reflects this ("which wins does not reduce to a single trait (r=0.29)... probe both per model").

(c) §2 line 146-147: "a content-type dissociation (meaning vs. surface form) that is model-specific." This is exactly the "semantic vs. surface" binary §6.5 overturns ("overturn the binary 'semantic (Gemma, Mistral) vs. surface (Qwen)' mode of earlier drafts", line 409; "no clean two-way semantic/surface mode", line 437). The corrected axis is token-presence↔structure (line 438, 445-447).

Confirmed §1 contributions (line 76-93) were updated to the token-presence / probe-per-model framing but §2 was not, leaving §2 stale and contradictory. Nothing in §2 caveats this. Given the paper's stated "methodological honesty" throughline, this is a genuine logic/consistency defect, not a false positive. The finding's cited line numbers are off by ~4 (actual §2 lines are 136-138 and 146-147) but the quoted text and section references are exact.

verdict.reason:
Both sub-claims verify against the paper's own evidence.

(1) Abstract coupling (lines 22-23): "recovers up to ~36% of a semantic context's value from a stripped cache (−3.8 nats on Gemma 27B)". Per §6.2, the 36% fraction is the Gemma 12B value — line 338 gives the sequence "6% → 23% → 36% → 35% (1B→4B→12B→27B), peaking at 12B", so the 27B fraction is 35%, not 36%. And −3.8 nats (table line 327: gemma27b sem-bank = 3.77) is the 27B absolute figure, whose fraction is 35%. So the abstract attaches a 27B nats number to a fraction (36%) that belongs to 12B, presenting them as one data point. The consistent pairing is "36% / −3.6 nats (12B, table shows 3.62)" or "35% / −3.8 nats (27B)". A real, minor internal-consistency imprecision. (A charitable "two separate up-to maxima" reading exists, but the parenthetical presentation does couple them.)

(2) "Universal" and "flip" sample basis: §6.4 rests on exactly three base models (line 207: "three pretrained base models (Gemma-4B-pt, Qwen-7B, Mistral-7B-v0.3)"; table lines 360-364). From these, line 368 asserts strongly and unhedged that "content imprinting is a universal property of pretrained LMs". The base→instruct "flip"/inversion is a headline contribution (lines 86, 369, 448, 630) but is demonstrated on only one model — Qwen 2.5 (line 369: "Qwen 2.5's tuning uniquely inverts it"). Section 10 (lines 590-613) caveats the token-presence/structure split, the select-vs-condition rule, downstream coverage, and synthetic facts, and notes they "do not identify which alignment objective changes it" — i.e., it caveats the mechanism, but not the n=3 basis for "universal" nor the n=1 basis for the "flip". Line 150 is a generic "one family may not transfer" hedge, not specific to these claims. So the finding is correct that these specific sample-size limits are uncaveated in §10.

Severity is minor, as flagged. The paper does not already handle either point.
verdict.revised_fix:
Two independent fixes in /home/jupyter/research/directed_kvcache_publication/paper_draft_v5.md:

1) Decouple the abstract's metric/model mismatch (lines 22-23). Either report both figures for one model — e.g. "recovers up to ~36% of a semantic context's value (−3.6 nats on Gemma 12B)" — or state them as two clearly-separate maxima: "recovers up to ~36% of a semantic context's value (peaking on Gemma 12B; the absolute banking peaks at −3.8 nats on Gemma 27B)". Do not attach the 27B nats number to the 12B 36% fraction as one point.

2) Add a one-line caveat to §10 (Limitations) noting the sample basis: the "universal property of pretrained LMs" claim rests on three base models (Gemma-4B, Qwen-7B, Mistral-7B), and the base→instruct inversion ("flip") is demonstrated on a single model (Qwen 2.5); broader base-model coverage and a second inverting family would strengthen both. Optionally soften "universal" at line 368 to e.g. "consistent across all three base models tested".

verdict.reason:
Confirmed real by reading the paper and running the cited evidence script. §7.1 (paper_draft_v5.md lines 534-539) explicitly "withdraw[s] the earlier 'decision rule indexed by imprinting character'" and prescribes "cheaply probe both operations per model," and §10 Limitations (lines 601-603) reiterates the comparison "does not yield a predictive rule ... which operation wins is model-specific and correlates only weakly with imprintability (r=0.29)." Yet §9 Practical Guidance item 3 (lines 580-584) reinstates exactly that withdrawn rule: "Choose the construction operation by imprinting character," keying Gemma->selection and Qwen-7B->condition, and re-uses the "weak/surface imprinter" label (line 582) that §6.5/§7 retired ("no clean semantic/surface mode," lines 410, 438, 477). This is a direct, unreconciled self-contradiction in the section a practitioner reads first.

The contradiction is also substantive, not merely rhetorical. Running make_taskaware_numbers.py reproduces r(imprintability,primeVal)=0.290 (matching the paper) with the script's own annotation "(NO clean trait law)" / "imprintability does NOT cleanly predict the direction." The data falsify §9 item 3's blanket claims: Gemma-1B (imprintability 0.43) has conditioning HELP strongly (primeVal=-1.908), not hurt as item 3 asserts for "Gemma"; and Qwen-14B (imprintability 0.39, essentially equal to Gemma-1B) has conditioning HURT massively (+2.369). So keying the operation to imprinting character is empirically wrong on the paper's own numbers. The paper nowhere caveats item 3 to reconcile it with §7.1/§10; item 3 states the rule affirmatively.

The finding's line-number references are slightly off from the current file (it cites §7.1 at 512-516 / item 3 at 557-561; in the current file the withdrawal is at 534-539, the table at 510-519, and item 3 at 580-584), but the sections, quoted text, and the r=0.29 evidence all match exactly. This is a genuine, critical narrative/consistency defect.
verdict.revised_fix:
Rewrite §9 Practical Guidance item 3 to match §7.1 and §10 — do NOT key the operation to imprinting character, and drop the "weak/surface imprinter" label. Replace with, e.g.:

"3. **When you know the task, probe both construction operations per model — do not assume one.** Which of task-aware *selection* (keep relevant tokens) vs. *conditioning* (discarded-task prime) wins is model-specific and does NOT reduce to imprintability (r=0.29 with conditioning value) or size: Gemma-1B and Qwen-14B sit at nearly equal imprintability yet have opposite outcomes. Run one cheap N~300 sweep of both operations before deploying. The one robust prior is negative: aggressive query-aware selection carries a Qwen-family-specific penalty (+0.9 to +1.9 nats, controlled for answer-span survival) that never appears on Gemma and that discarded-prefix conditioning avoids — so if you must deploy a single operation blind on a Qwen model, prefer conditioning over SnapKV-style selection."

This keeps the genuinely supported empirical prior (the Qwen selection penalty, §7.1 third reading) and the warning against blind single-operation deployment, while removing the withdrawn imprinting-character law and the retired surface-imprinter framing.

verdict.reason:
Confirmed real internal contradiction, verified against the paper and the source data. §9 Practical Guidance item 3 (paper_draft_v5.md lines 579-583) instructs the reader to "Choose the construction operation by imprinting character," asserting "on a high-imprintability token-presence model (Gemma), conditioning hurts — prefer selection" and "on a weak/surface imprinter where selection hurts (Qwen-7B), condition instead." This directly resurrects a decision rule that the paper explicitly WITHDRAWS in three other places: §7.1 (lines 533-538: "we therefore withdraw the earlier 'decision rule indexed by imprinting character'; the deployable prescription is ... cheaply probe both operations per model"), the abstract (line 50: "we withdraw the 'rule indexed by imprinting' and conclude you must probe both per model"), and the conclusion (lines 626-629: "which wins is model-specific and does not reduce to imprintability or size, so the honest prescription is to probe both per model"). Limitations (lines 597-599) likewise reaffirms "does not yield a predictive rule."

The rule is also factually refuted by the paper's own §7.1 table (lines 511-518), which I verified against the source experiments/17_taskaware/FINDINGS.md (lines 44-53): (a) §9.3 claims conditioning hurts Gemma, but Gemma-1B has the LARGEST conditioning help in the table (full conditioning/primeVal = -1.91*); (b) §9.3 says a weak imprinter where selection hurts should condition, but selection hurts all Qwens including Qwen-14B (selVal +0.88*), whose conditioning HURTS most of any model (+2.37*) — the exact counterexample §7.1 uses to withdraw the rule. Gemma-1B (imp 0.43) and Qwen-14B (imp 0.39) sit at nearly equal imprintability yet have opposite-sign conditioning, which is why the paper elsewhere abandons the imprinting-indexed rule (r=0.29).

The finding's cited line numbers are slightly off (the content is at lines 579-583 and 536, not 557 and 514), but the quoted text and all numbers are correct and present. §9.3 does contain a partial hedge ("Do not deploy a single operation blind..."), but the item's headline instruction and framing still tell the reader to choose the operation by imprinting character, so this does not caveat away the contradiction — §9.3 remains the sole outlier against the abstract, §7.1, conclusion, and limitations.
verdict.revised_fix: Rewrite §9 Practical Guidance item 3 (paper_draft_v5.md lines 579-583) to match §7.1/abstract/conclusion/limitations. Replace with, e.g.: "**Do not choose the construction operation by a trait shortcut — probe both per model.** For extraction with a known task, neither task-aware selection (keep relevant tokens) nor discarded-prefix conditioning dominates, and which wins does not reduce to imprintability (r=0.29) or size — Gemma-1B (conditioning helps most, -1.9) and Qwen-14B (conditioning hurts most, +2.4) sit at nearly the same imprintability. Run one cheap (N approximately 300) per-model sweep of both operations rather than assuming one. The only reliable prior is confound-controlled on the selection side: aggressive query-aware (SnapKV-style) selection carries a Qwen-family penalty (+0.9 to +1.9 nats, even at matched answer-span survival), so avoid selection-only pipelines on Qwen; conditioning sidesteps this." Remove the "by imprinting character" framing and the false "on Gemma conditioning hurts" / "on Qwen condition instead" assignments entirely.

finding.location: §9 Practical Guidance, item 3, line 557: "**Choose the construction operation by imprinting character, not by default-priming.** For extraction with a known task: on a high-imprintability token-presence model (Gemma), conditioning *hurts* — prefer task-aware **selection** ... on a weak/surface imprinter where selection *hurts* (Qwen-7B), **condition** instead."

The abstract (line 46, "we withdraw the 'rule indexed by imprinting'") and conclusion (line 607, "probe both per model") agree with §7.1. §9.3 directly contradicts all three by telling the reader to choose the operation by imprinting character. Worse, the rule as stated is refuted by the paper's own data: it says "on a high-imprintability token-presence model (Gemma), conditioning hurts," but Gemma-1B (a Gemma) has the LARGEST conditioning HELP (full conditioning −1.91*, §7.1 table); and it says a weak imprinter where selection hurts should "condition instead," but selection hurts ALL Qwens including Qwen-14B, whose conditioning HURTS most (+2.37*) — the exact counterexample §7.1 uses to withdraw the rule.
finding.evidence: §7.1 line 511-516 withdraws the 'decision rule indexed by imprinting character' because r(imprintability,conditioning)=0.29 and 'Gemma-1B (the strongest help, −1.9) and Qwen-14B (the strongest hurt, +2.4) sit at nearly the same imprintability.' Source exp31b confirms: gemma3_1b primeVal=−1.91*, qwen25_14b primeVal=+2.37*, imprintability 0.43 vs 0.39. §9.3's rule assigns 'Gemma → conditioning hurts' (false for Gemma-1B) and 'Qwen → condition' (false for Qwen-14B).
finding.fix: Rewrite §9.3 to match §7.1/abstract/conclusion: do NOT choose by imprinting character. The only reliable prior is that aggressive query-aware selection carries a Qwen-family penalty, so avoid selection-only pipelines on Qwen.' Remove the 'by imprinting character' framing entirely.

verdict.reason: Genuine internal contradiction, verified against the paper's own text and data. (1) §9 point 3's heading "Choose the construction operation **by imprinting character**" (line 580) is near-verbatim the rule §7.1 explicitly withdraws: line 537 "we therefore **withdraw** the earlier 'decision rule indexed by imprinting character'." (2) The trait-indexed framing contradicts the paper's own quantitative verdict repeated in two places: §7.1 line 534-537 and §10 line 601-603 both state the operation winner does NOT reduce to imprintability (r=0.29) or size and "requires an empirical per-model probe." (3) Point 3's routing is falsified by the paper's OWN table (line 516): Gemma-1B (imprintability 0.43) is the single biggest conditioning *winner* (COND|sel −1.05, full −1.91), yet point 3 says "on a ... Gemma, conditioning *hurts* — prefer selection." Gemma-1B and Qwen-14B sit at nearly identical imprintability with opposite conditioning signs (this exact pair is cited in §7.1 line 535-536 as the reason to withdraw the rule). (4) "weak/surface imprinter" (line 582) reuses the retracted "semantic vs. surface" mode vocabulary that §6.5 explicitly overturns (lines 25, 410, 438); instruct-Qwen is characterized as banking "little" (weak), while "surface/code" is attributed to Qwen's *base* model (line 39), so "surface imprinter" for Qwen-7B is retracted terminology. The partial hedge in point 3's last sentence ("Do not deploy a single operation blind") does not rescue it because it is subordinate to a heading and prescription that reinstate the withdrawn trait-index; the paper does NOT caveat this contradiction — it directly conflicts with the honest verdict stated elsewhere.
verdict.revised_fix: Rewrite §9 point 3 to match §7.1's and §10's honest verdict. Replace the heading "Choose the construction operation by imprinting character, not by default-priming." with something trait-agnostic, e.g. "**When you know the task, probe both operations per model — do not assume selection or conditioning.**" Body: state that across eight models neither operation dominates (conditioning helps four, hurts four) and which wins does NOT reduce to imprintability (r=0.29) or size — e.g. Gemma-1B (low imprintability) is the strongest conditioning winner while similarly-imprintable Qwen-14B is the strongest loser — so deployment requires a cheap per-model N≈300 select-vs-condition sweep. Remove "by imprinting character," the "high-imprintability token-presence model (Gemma) → prefer selection" routing (contradicted by Gemma-1B in the paper's own table; if kept, scope it to the larger Gemmas, not "Gemma"), and the retracted "weak/surface imprinter" phrasing. Retain only the genuinely systematic, confound-controlled claim that aggressive query-aware selection hurts the entire Qwen family (not Gemma) even at matched answer-span survival, so conditioning is the safer default specifically for Qwen.

finding.location: §9 Practical Guidance, point 3, line 557: "**Choose the construction operation by imprinting character, not by default-priming.**"
finding.issue: This prescription contradicts §7.1, which explicitly WITHDRAWS exactly this rule: line 514 "we therefore **withdraw** the earlier 'decision rule indexed by imprinting character'" and line 511-512 "the choice does *not* reduce to a single trait ... (r=0.29)". §10 Limitations (line 575) also states the comparison "does not yield a predictive rule." Point 3 reinstates the withdrawn trait-indexed rule as deployable guidance and even routes by "weak/surface imprinter" (surface = retracted mode vocab, line 559).
finding.fix: Rewrite point 3 to match §7.1's honest verdict: do not choose the operation by any trait; because neither selection nor conditioning dominates and the winner does not reduce to imprintability/size (r=0.29), cheaply probe both operations per model (one N≈300 sweep). Remove "by imprinting character" and "weak/surface imprinter."

verdict.reason: Verified against the paper. §3.3 (actual line 207, not 202) reads: "plus three pretrained **base** models (Gemma-4B-pt, Qwen-7B, Mistral-7B-v0.3)". The finding's facts are accurate: two of the three base entries carry disambiguating base markers (Gemma "-pt" = pretrained; Mistral "-v0.3" = the base version, whose instruct counterpart is Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3), but the Qwen base entry is written as bare "Qwen-7B". That exact bare token is the paper's standard name for the Qwen INSTRUCT model everywhere else: the same sentence's opening (line 206) lists "Eight instruction-tuned models ... Qwen 2.5 (1.5/7/14B)"; the imprintability table (line 325, qwen7b 0.37) is an instruct model; §7/§7.1 (lines 476, 514) use "Qwen-7B" for the instruct model; and §6.4's own table (lines 362-363) explicitly disambiguates "Qwen-7B base" vs "Qwen-7B instruct". So within a single list the bare "Qwen-7B" is inconsistent with its two suffixed companions and collides with the instruct-model name. The only mitigating context is the introducing phrase "three pretrained base models," which signals all three are base — this softens but does not remove the within-list inconsistency, and the paper adds no explicit caveat. Genuine but minor/cosmetic terminology nit.
verdict.revised_fix: In the §3.3 base-model list (line 207), give the Qwen base a suffix consistent with the paper's own conventions. Best is "Qwen-7B-pt" to directly parallel the sibling "Gemma-4B-pt", giving "(Gemma-4B-pt, Qwen-7B-pt, Mistral-7B-v0.3)". Alternatively "Qwen-7B base" to match §6.4's table wording. Either removes the collision with the instruct-model name used elsewhere.

finding.location: §3.3 line 202: "three pretrained **base** models (Gemma-4B-pt, Qwen-7B, Mistral-7B-v0.3)"

finding.fix: Write the base Qwen with a suffix consistent with the others, e.g. "Qwen-7B-base" (or "Qwen2.5-7B-pt") in the §3.3 list.

verdict.reason: Verified against the paper's own reproducible source-of-truth script (make_numbers.py), whose star convention is defined at line 15 as `'*' if (t[1]>0 or t[2]<0)` — i.e., a star whenever the 95% bootstrap CI excludes 0, regardless of sign. §3.3 line 210 states the same: "`*` excludes 0." I re-ran the exact section-6 computation (base vs instruct, exp26). The code column yields: gemma3_4b_base +0.313 [+0.295,+0.331]*, gemma3_4b +0.151 [+0.038,+0.265]*, qwen25_7b_base +0.169 [+0.136,+0.202]*, mistral_7b_base −0.162 [−0.187,−0.139]* — all four CIs exclude 0 and are significant. The paper's §6.4 table (lines 360-364) omits stars on exactly these four cells while starring the two negative ones (qwen −0.37*, mistral −0.56*). This is internally inconsistent: the star convention is applied uniformly everywhere else in the paper for BOTH signs — e.g., §6.1 line 292 stars a significant positive (qwen rare_word +0.18*), and §6.5/§8 tables (lines 400-407, 512-515) star significant positives like +0.47*, +1.43*, +1.89*, +2.54* while marking non-significant values "(n.s.)". Within §6.4 itself, mistral_7b_base −0.16 (code) lacks a star while its counterpart mistral_7b −0.56* (code) has one, despite both being significant and same interpretation category. The paper contains no note exempting the code column from starring; the parentheticals (no)/(code)/(none) are interpretation labels (whether code is banked), orthogonal to significance. So a reader cannot tell these base-model code effects are statistically significant. It is a genuine (though correctly minor/cosmetic) numerics/notation inconsistency. The proposed fix numbers match the regeneration exactly.
verdict.revised_fix: Add the significance star to the four significant code-column cells in the §6.4 table (lines 360-364), preserving the existing interpretation parentheticals: change "+0.31 (no)" → "+0.31\* (no)" (Gemma-4B base), "+0.15 (no)" → "+0.15\* (no)" (Gemma-4B instruct), "+0.17 (no)" → "+0.17\* (no)" (Qwen-7B base), and "−0.16 (code)" → "−0.16\* (code)" (Mistral-7B base). This makes the "`*` excludes 0" convention uniform across the table and the rest of the paper. (All four CIs from make_numbers.py exclude 0: +0.313 [+0.295,+0.331], +0.151 [+0.038,+0.265], +0.169 [+0.136,+0.202], −0.162 [−0.187,−0.139].)

finding.location: §6.4 table, lines 355-360: code-bank column entries "Gemma-4B base +0.31 (no)", "Gemma-4B instruct +0.15 (no)", "Qwen-7B base +0.17 (no)", "Mistral-7B base −0.16 (code)"
finding.issue: Per the paper's stated convention (§3.3 line 206: "`*` excludes 0"), four code-column cells that are statistically significant in the source lack the significance star, while the two negative ones (Qwen-7B instruct −0.37*, Mistral instruct −0.56*) carry it. This is internally inconsistent: §6.1 correctly stars a significant POSITIVE value (Qwen rare_word +0.18*), so significant positives elsewhere should also be starred. As written, a reader cannot tell these base-model code effects are significant.
finding.evidence: make_numbers regen: gemma3_4b_base code +0.313 [+0.295,+0.331]*, gemma3_4b code +0.151 [+0.038,+0.265]*, qwen25_7b_base code +0.169 [+0.136,+0.202]*, mistral_7b_base code −0.162 [−0.187,−0.139]* — all CIs exclude 0. Paper omits stars on all four.
finding.fix: Add stars to the four significant code cells (Gemma-4B base +0.31\*, Gemma-4B instruct +0.15\*, Qwen-7B base +0.17\*, Mistral-7B base −0.16\*) so the star convention is applied uniformly.

verdict.reason: Verified against source-of-truth make_numbers.py §1 (recomputed from results/exp10_boolq/*/results.json). The numbers in the paper are exactly right: gemma-12B gold=yes +0.916*/gold=no +0.782* (both up, both sig), qwen-7B +0.390*/+0.489* (both up, both sig), ministral-8B +0.450* / +0.001 with CI [-0.037,+0.039] (one class up, one class null), qwen-1.5B +0.403*/-0.326* (up/down = pure prior shift). §3.2 (lines 190-192) explicitly defines the control: 'both classes up is genuine discrimination'; a symmetric up/down split is a label-prior shift. The §4 sentence (lines 235-238) partitions the models into exactly two buckets — a 'real discrimination gain' group (gemma-12B, qwen-7B, ministral-8B) and 'only qwen-1.5B shows the pure prior shift' — thereby forcing ministral-8B into the 'real discrimination' bucket. But ministral-8B satisfies neither category cleanly: it is not both-classes-up (gold=no is null, CI includes 0), and it is not the symmetric up/down prior shift. It is a genuine intermediate/one-sided case (yes up, no flat; balanced accuracy does rise 0.850->0.863, so not a pure prior shift either). This is inconsistent with the section's own both-classes-up definition, and the paper does not caveat ministral's null gold=no anywhere (only other ministral mentions are about structure-banking in §6). So the flag is a real, minor logic inconsistency, not a false positive. The severity is correctly 'minor' — the qualitative claim (extract sharpens rather than merely shifts the prior on capable models) still holds for the two models that fully meet the criterion.
verdict.revised_fix: In §4 (line ~237) do not list ministral-8B as a full 'real discrimination gain' example alongside gemma-12B and qwen-7B. Instead note it as an intermediate/one-sided case and adjust the closing partition so it doesn't imply 'only qwen-1.5B' departs from full discrimination. Concretely, change '...qwen-7B +0.390*/+0.489*; ministral-8B +0.450*/+0.001) and improves accuracy, ECE, and Brier; only qwen-1.5B shows the pure prior shift (+0.40/-0.33).' to something like: '...qwen-7B +0.390*/+0.489*), both clearing the both-classes-up bar, and improves accuracy, ECE, and Brier; ministral-8B is an intermediate case (gold=yes +0.450*, gold=no +0.001 n.s. — one class up, one flat), improving balanced accuracy (0.850->0.863) without the symmetric up/down signature; only qwen-1.5B shows the pure prior shift (+0.40/-0.33).' This keeps the headline claim (discrimination sharpening on the two models that meet the criterion) while making ministral's partial/null gold=no explicit and consistent with §3.2.

finding.location: §4, line 235-237: "extract priming produces a *real* discrimination gain on the larger instruct models (gemma-12B ... ; qwen-7B +0.390*/+0.489*; ministral-8B +0.450*/+0.001) and improves accuracy, ECE, and Brier; only qwen-1.5B shows the pure prior shift"
finding.issue: Ministral-8B is grouped with the 'real discrimination gain' models, but its gold=no split is +0.001 (n.s.), i.e. one class up (+0.450*) and the other flat. By the paper's own §3.2 criterion (line 190-192: 'both classes up is genuine discrimination'; a one-sided move is not), Ministral-8B does NOT satisfy 'both classes up' — it is closer to a one-sided / partial-prior pattern than to the genuine-discrimination pattern that gemma-12B (+0.916/+0.782) and qwen-7B (+0.390/+0.489) exhibit. Listing it as a 'real discrimination gain' example is inconsistent with the section's own definition.
finding.evidence: Source (make_numbers.py §1): ministral_8b gold=yes +0.450*, gold=no +0.001 (CI [-0.037,+0.039] includes 0). §3.2 line 190-192 defines both-classes-up as the genuine-discrimination signature.
finding.fix: Either move ministral-8B out of the 'real discrimination gain' list (note it as an intermediate/one-sided case: yes up, no flat), or add a clause acknowledging its gold=no is null so it only partially meets the both-up criterion.

verdict.reason: Verified against the paper. Three distinct adjectives are used for the closely-related idea of cancelling the reposition+normalize "construction/machinery" cost, and only one is defined. "machinery-neutral" is defined at §3.2 (line 198-199) as the content-free newline prime/control, and used consistently in that sense (lines 17, 261, 575). But "machinery-controlled" (§6.1 line 285, §7 line 464, appendix line 665) and "machinery-matched" (§7.1 line 506) appear inline as measurement adjectives with no definition and no note that they relate to the machinery-neutral baseline. A reader must infer that "machinery-controlled" means "measured relative to the machinery-neutral prime." So the core complaint — undefined synonyms for a defined concept — is genuine and the "minor" severity is right. One nuance the finding gets slightly wrong: "machinery-matched" at line 506 is not the newline-prime subtraction; it labels a same-machinery difference (COND|sel = conditioning the selected set minus selection-alone, "retained set held fixed"), where the construction cost cancels because both terms share it, not because the neutral prime is subtracted. It still shares the "machinery cancelled" theme and is still undefined, so it belongs in the same fix, but it is not a strict synonym of the neutral-prime baseline. The paper nowhere caveats or cross-references these terms, so it does not already handle the issue.
verdict.revised_fix: In §3.2, extend the machinery-neutral bullet with a defining clause that names the measurement adjectives, e.g.: "Machinery-neutral prime — a content-free, length-matched prime (newlines) isolates the reposition+normalize construction cost from the prime's content effect. Throughout, an effect reported as 'machinery-controlled' has this construction cost subtracted (measured relative to the machinery-neutral prime); a difference reported as 'machinery-matched' cancels the construction cost because both compared conditions share identical machinery (e.g., COND|sel in §7.1, where the retained set is held fixed)." Alternatively, standardize on a single adjective ("machinery-controlled") wherever the newline-prime baseline is subtracted (§6.1 line 285, §7 line 464, appendix line 665), while keeping "machinery-matched" only for the same-machinery difference at §7.1 line 506 since it is a genuinely different construction — but in that case still define both terms at §3.2.

finding.location: "machinery-neutral" (§3.2 line 193, §5 line 256, §9 line 552) vs "machinery-controlled" (§6.1 line 280, §7 line 441, §B line 636) vs "machinery-matched" (§7.1 line 483)
finding.issue: Three different adjectives for one concept (subtracting the content-free reposition+normalize construction cost via the newline prime). Only "machinery-neutral prime" is defined (§3.2 line 193). The measurement adjectives "machinery-controlled" and "machinery-matched" are never defined and never noted as synonyms of the neutral-prime baseline, so the reader must infer they all denote the same subtraction.
finding.fix: Pick one adjective for the measurement basis (e.g. "machinery-controlled") and use it consistently, or add a clause at §3.2 stating that "machinery-controlled / machinery-matched" means "measured relative to the machinery-neutral prime."

verdict.reason: Verified against the paper. "Imprintability" is first bolded/introduced in the Abstract (line 22) and Introduction (line 81-82), but both only gloss what it *predicts* (banking magnitude, "recovers up to ~36% of a semantic context's value" / "a single trait ... that scales with model size") — not its operational metric. It is then used quantitatively well before definition: §3.3 line 206 ranks the model set "spanning imprintability," and §4 line 234 splits results into "low-imprintability models (Qwen-1.5B, Mistral-7B)" vs "high-imprintability Gemma" with specific margin numbers attached. The operational definition — "mean |Δ query-NLL| a generic prefix induces" — appears only at §6.2 line 318, roughly 110 lines after the §3.3/§4 uses. The finding's characterization that the Abstract gloss describes what the metric predicts rather than defining it is correct, and there is no forward reference at any early use, so the paper does not already handle/caveat this. A reader hitting the §4 low/high split has no operational meaning for the axis. The severity is appropriately "minor" (direction is partly inferable from the model labels and the "scales with size" gloss), but it is a genuine term-before-definition issue.
verdict.revised_fix: Add a one-clause operational gloss at the first quantitative use, §3.3 line 206, and forward-reference the full definition. Change "Eight instruction-tuned models spanning imprintability: Qwen 2.5..." to "Eight instruction-tuned models spanning imprintability (mean |Δ query-NLL| a generic prefix induces; defined §6.2): Qwen 2.5...". This grounds both the §3.3 ordering and the §4 low/high-imprintability split. Optionally, add the same short parenthetical at the Introduction's first bolded use (line 81) for readers who skip to it, though the §3.3 insertion is the load-bearing fix since it precedes the §4 quantitative split.

finding.location: §3.3 line 201 and §4 lines 228-229 use "imprintability" quantitatively before its metric definition in §6.2 line 313 ("Define **imprintability** as the mean |Δ query-NLL| a generic prefix induces")
finding.issue: Term used before definition. Models are ranked by "imprintability" in §3.3 ("Eight instruction-tuned models spanning imprintability") and §4 splits results into "low-imprintability" vs "high-imprintability" models (lines 228-229) with quantitative force, but the metric is only defined in §6.2. The Abstract gloss (line 22-23, "recovers up to ~36%...") describes what imprintability predicts, not the metric itself, so it is not a definition. A reader hits the §4 low/high split with no operational meaning of the axis.
finding.fix: Add a one-clause operational definition at first quantitative use (§3.3), e.g. "...spanning imprintability (mean |Δ query-NLL| from a generic prefix; defined §6.2)...", so §4's low/high-imprintability split is grounded.

verdict.reason: Verified against the source. The §4 cascade paragraph (lines 247-251) enumerates three "clean claims that fell to the controls": (1) contrastive keyword construction adds nothing over plain passage keywords (neighbor-subtraction inert, n.s. on 4 models); (2) its "cacheable" variant was 85% leaked from the candidate set; (3) a borderline win at N=300 failed to replicate at N=400 before re-emerging at N=900. The sentence ends "We report these in §8" — plural "these" referring to all three. §8 (lines 555-570, "What Did Not Pan Out") contains four bullets: contrastive priming inert (matches casualty #1 only), content-coherence positional artifact, five architectural accounts falsified, and no universal win/precise-fact injection. A grep across the entire paper confirms "85%"/"leaked"/"cacheable" appears ONLY at line 249 (the §4 sentence itself) and nowhere in §8, and the "N=300→400→900 re-emergence" story appears only at lines 249-250 (line 502 and 657 use N=300/N=900 for unrelated experiments — task-aware selection and banking magnitude). So two of the three items promised by the §8 pointer are genuinely absent from §8; the pointer is partially dangling. The paper does not caveat this anywhere. This is a real (if minor) cross-reference defect.
verdict.revised_fix: Soften the §4 pointer so it references only the item §8 actually reports. Change the final sentence of the cascade paragraph (line 251) from "We report these in §8." to "We report the contrastive-priming result in §8." Alternatively (more invasive), add two bullets to §8 for the 85%-leaked cacheable variant and the N=300/400/900 replication flip so that the plural "these" is satisfied. The softening option is lower-risk and preferred since it does not require surfacing the underlying stats in §8.

finding.location: §4, lines 242-246 (cascade paragraph) ending "...trust only high-powered estimates. We report these in §8."
finding.issue: The sentence promises §8 covers all three cascade casualties (contrastive/neighbor-subtraction inertness; the '85% leaked cacheable variant'; the N=300→400→900 replication flip), but §8 (lines 532-546) only reports the neighbor-subtraction inertness ('Contrastive priming is inert'). The 85%-leaked cacheable variant and the N=300/400/900 re-emergence story do not appear in §8, so the pointer is partially dangling.
finding.evidence: §8 bullets cover: contrastive priming inert, coherence positional artifact, five architectural accounts, no universal win/precise-fact injection. No mention of '85% leaked' or 'N=300/400/900'.
finding.fix: Either add the leaked-cacheable and N-scaling replication items to §8, or soften the §4 sentence to 'We report the contrastive-priming result in §8' rather than 'these'.

verdict.reason:
Verified against the paper. The factual claims in the finding hold: §6 is titled "...(the central result)" (line 273) and is immediately followed by a "**Reading guide / correction.**" box (lines 275-281) that narrates and retracts the earlier "semantic imprinting"/"semantic vs. surface mode" framing before the section states any current claim. The same retraction is genuinely repeated elsewhere: §6.1's body (lines 296-308), §6.5 (lines 410, 438, 449), and the conclusion (§11, lines 622-623), plus the abstract (lines 9, 25) and §1.2 (lines 85-87). So the top-of-section box is redundant with the correction already carried in §6.1's opening paragraph directly below it, and §6.1's own subsection heading ("a content-imprint axis (magnitude) with a model-specific character") already states the corrected thesis. The narrative weakness the finding describes is real: leading the flagship section with a boxed self-retraction, when the same retraction is restated immediately below and four more times, reads defensively and buries the current thesis.

Two caveats that temper severity from "major" to moderate: (1) This paper's entire framing is an intentional, coherent "honest self-correction" narrative — the title is already the corrected term ("Content Imprinting"), the abstract opens with "our own earlier work overstated it," and corrections are a deliberate through-line, not an accidental changelog leak. So the correction *content* is load-bearing and should not be excised, only relocated. (2) The finding contains one minor citation error: it labels line 596 as "§11" but that line is under "## 10. Limitations" (§11 is the Conclusion at line 617); this does not affect the substance. The paper does not already resolve the specific defect (the redundant top-of-section box is present as flagged), so real=true, but the fix should preserve the intentional correction narrative rather than reduce it to a lone footnote.
verdict.revised_fix: Remove the standalone "**Reading guide / correction.**" box at the top of §6 (lines 275-281) and let §6.1 open the section, since §6.1's heading already states the corrected thesis and its body (lines 296-308) already carries the full retraction. Optionally retitle §6 to drop the self-aggrandizing "(the central result)" parenthetical, or fold the one genuinely useful pointer from the box — "magnitude results are robust; the *kind* of banking is reinterpreted in §6.5" — into the first sentence of §6.1 as a forward reference rather than a boxed retraction. Do NOT reduce the correction to a single footnote at §6.5: this paper's contribution is explicitly framed as an honest self-correction (abstract line 9, §1.2, conclusion), so the retraction should remain visible in the abstract, §6.1, and §6.5 — the fix is only to eliminate the duplicated top-of-section banner, not the correction narrative itself.

finding.location: §6 header + boxed note (lines 268-276): "## 6. Imprinting: a Content-Imprint Axis, and What It Actually Banks (the central result)" immediately followed by "> **Reading guide / correction.** An earlier framing of this section called the headline axis 'semantic imprinting' and split models into 'semantic vs. surface' *modes*. Word-order shuffle controls (§6.5) show that label is too strong ..."
finding.issue: The central results section opens by narrating its own prior draft's error before stating the current result. Placing a 'correction' box at the top of the flagship section makes the visible course-correction structural rather than incidental — it reads as a changelog entry, not a paper. A reviewer encountering 'the central result' section that begins by apologizing for a previous framing will discount the section before reading the evidence.
finding.fix: Delete the 'Reading guide / correction' box from the top of §6. State the corrected claim directly as the section's thesis: 'Priming banks context with a magnitude set by one trait (imprintability); what is banked is model-specific — token presence for Gemma, structure for Mistral (§6.5).' If any acknowledgment of the earlier 'semantic mode' framing is needed, place a single footnote at §6.5 (where the shuffle controls that justify the correction actually appear), not a banner at the section head.
finding.evidence: Line 268 labels §6 'the central result'; lines 270-276 spend six lines describing and retracting an earlier framing before any current claim. The same retraction is then repeated at §6.1 (lines 292-303), §6.5 (lines 397, 426), and §11 (line 596) — four separate retractions of one framing.

verdict.reason: Verified against the primary evidence (results/exp31_taskaware_qa/*/results.json, N=300 SQuAD). My independent bootstrap reproduces the paper's §7 table (line 470): the "order contribution" = sem_prime − surf_prime is +0.04 [−0.12,+0.19] n.s. (Gemma-12B), +0.16 [−0.01,+0.33] n.s. (Gemma-4B), −0.07 [−0.17,+0.03] n.s. (Qwen-7B), and +0.163 [+0.028,+0.304] SIGNIFICANT (Qwen-14B). So the paper's own table marks Qwen-14B order as "+0.16*", yet the leading sentence at line 473 states categorically "The mechanism is **not** semantic blurring" across all four models. For Qwen-14B, order does contribute significantly, so the strict categorical framing overreaches for that one model. The parenthetical hedge "order contributes ≤0.16 nats, mostly n.s." is honest and the word "mostly" partially mitigates, but the paper never names the Qwen-14B exception in prose and the categorical lead sentence is in tension with its own table's asterisk. Severity is correctly "minor": the underlying science still holds (even for Qwen-14B the shuffled prime retains +0.28 of the +0.44 effect, ~64% token presence), so "predominantly token presence" is defensible; only the unqualified categorical wording is the issue.
verdict.revised_fix: Soften the categorical lead at line 473 to name the exception explicitly, e.g.: "The mechanism is **predominantly token presence, not semantic blurring**: shuffling the question's tokens leaves most of the effect intact (order contributes ≤0.16 nats and is n.s. for three of four models — significant only for Qwen-14B, +0.16*, where even so the shuffled prime still carries the majority, +0.28 of +0.44). What moves the answer is chiefly the token presence of the question in the cached representation, with a sign that is model-and-scale-specific." verdict.reason: Genuine internal contradiction / overclaim. Section 7.1 explicitly withdraws the trait-indexed decision rule (lines 534-539: "We therefore **withdraw** the earlier 'decision rule indexed by imprinting character'; the deployable prescription is the weaker but honest *cheaply probe both operations per model*... not a trait shortcut"), and §10 Limitations reiterates it (lines 601-604: "does not yield a predictive rule... deployment requires an empirical per-model probe"). But Practical Guidance point 3 (actual lines 580-584, not 557-561 as the finding states — those are §8) reinstates exactly that rule: its heading is literally "Choose the construction operation **by imprinting character**," and it instructs "on a high-imprintability token-presence model (Gemma), conditioning *hurts* — prefer task-aware selection... or no priming." This is unconditional and uncaveated. It is falsified by the paper's own data: the §7.1 table (line 516) shows Gemma-1B full conditioning = -1.91, the single strongest HELP of all eight models (7.1 itself calls it "the strongest *help*, -1.9"), and 7.1 stresses conditioning value does NOT track imprintability (r=0.29), so grouping all "Gemma" as a case where conditioning hurts is wrong. Gemma-1B (imprint. 0.43) also isn't "high-imprintability" — it's mid-range (0.20-0.84). The paper does not caveat this in 9.3, so real=true. (Note: the finding's line numbers are off by ~23 and it slightly mischaracterizes the harm as being "conditioning helps most" being the falsifier — that part is correct — but the location "Section 9 point 3" is unambiguous and the substance is right.)
verdict.revised_fix: Rewrite Practical Guidance point 3 to match §7.1's actual conclusion instead of reinstating the withdrawn trait rule. Drop the "by imprinting character" framing and the blanket "on Gemma, conditioning hurts" claim. Replace with: (a) the one systematic, confound-controlled result — aggressive query-aware (SnapKV-style) selection hurts the whole Qwen family (+0.9 to +1.9 nats) but never Gemma, so selection carries a real Qwen-specific risk; and (b) for the select-vs-condition choice generally, state that neither operation dominates and which wins does not track imprintability or size (r=0.29; e.g., Gemma-1B is the strongest conditioning help at -1.91 while similar-imprintability Qwen-14B is the strongest hurt at +2.4), so run one cheap N~300 per-model probe rather than picking by family/trait. This keeps 9.3 consistent with §7.1 (lines 534-539) and §10 (lines 601-604).

finding.location: Section 9 point 3 lines 557-561
finding.issue: Section 9.3 reinstates the per-family rule that section 7.1 withdraws, falsified by Gemma-1B where conditioning helps most at minus 1.91.
finding.fix: Rewrite to probe both per model.

verdict.reason:
Verified against the paper body and the reframe memory (taskaware_and_shuffle_reframe.md). The two flagged Section 2 (Related Work) sentences are genuine residual overclaims that lag those corrections and contradict them:
(1) Line 137-139: "Our contribution to this thread is the *mode* account: which conditioning content a given model can bank is governed by a measurable trait (§6.2), so the right task-aware prime is *model-specific* — a prescription these retention-based methods do not provide." Two caveats on the finding: (a) the cited line numbers are slightly off — the actual overclaiming phrases sit at 137-139 and 147-150, while the finding cites 132-134 and 142-144 (neutral prior-art setup lines), though it points to the right two paragraphs; (b) severity is overstated as "major" — the abstract, intro, contributions, and all body sections handle the reframe correctly, so this is an internal-consistency lag in the Related Work summary only (minor/moderate). It is still a real fix because these sentences do sell a retracted framing to a §2 reader. Note also that the §6.4 base→instruct "content-type flip" that line 148 points to IS a genuine, retained result, so the fix must keep that while dropping the disavowed "meaning vs. surface" label and the "mode/model-specific-prescription" framing.

verdict.reason: Confirmed real by independent recomputation from exp26 raw data. The abstract (lines 22-23) writes "it recovers up to ~36% of a semantic context's value from a stripped cache (−3.8 nats on Gemma 27B)", which binds the peak fraction (~36%) to Gemma-27B via the parenthetical. But the body §6.2 line 338 states the fraction sequence is "6% → 23% → 36% → 35% (1B→4B→12B→27B), peaking at 12B" — so ~36% is the 12B value, not 27B. My recomputation from results/exp26_bank_semantic confirms this exactly: fraction = banking(sem_strip−sem_neutral) / full_value(sem_retain−sem_neutral) gives 1B=6.8%, 4B=23.8%, 12B=37.6% (the peak), 27B=32.8%. The −3.8 nats is correctly 27B's ABSOLUTE banking (sem_strip−sem_neutral = −3.770), but 27B's FRACTION is ~33%, not 36%. So the abstract conflates 12B's peak fraction with 27B's absolute nats, contradicting the body. The "rising monotonically in nats" clause is accurate (nats: −0.57 → −2.46 → −3.62 → −3.77) and does not rescue the mis-attributed fraction. The paper does not caveat this; the body flatly contradicts the abstract's implied binding. This is a genuine numerics/attribution inconsistency in a headline abstract sentence, hence major.
verdict.revised_fix:
Decouple the peak fraction from Gemma-27B so the abstract matches the body (§6.2, "peaking at 12B"). Replace lines 22-23:

"it recovers up to ~36% of a semantic context's value from a stripped cache (−3.8 nats on Gemma 27B), rising monotonically in nats across the Gemma family,"

with:

"it recovers up to ~36% of a semantic context's value from a stripped cache (peaking at Gemma-12B; the largest absolute effect is −3.8 nats on Gemma-27B), rising monotonically in nats across the Gemma family,"

This preserves the accurate ~36% peak fraction and the accurate −3.8-nat/27B absolute figure while removing the false implication that ~36% is 27B's fraction.

verdict.reason:
Verified against the paper. The Related Work paragraph (lines 137-139) states: "Our contribution to this thread is the *mode* account: which conditioning content a given model can bank is governed by a measurable trait (§6.2), so the right task-aware prime is *model-specific* — a prescription these retention-based methods do not provide." This over-claims relative to the corrected body on two counts, and — unlike every other summarizing section — was not reconciled to the retractions:

1) "the *mode* account": §6.5 explicitly retires the mode framing. Line 410 says the new three-way taxonomy "overturns the binary 'semantic vs. surface' mode of earlier drafts," and line 438 concludes "there is, in short, no clean two-way semantic/surface mode." The Related Work still sells "the mode account" as the headline contribution.

2) "the right task-aware prime is *model-specific* — a prescription these retention-based methods do not provide": This is precisely the prescription the paper withdraws. §7.1 (lines 534-539) finds conditioning value barely correlates with imprintability (r=0.29) and states "we therefore **withdraw** the earlier 'decision rule indexed by imprinting character'; the deployable prescription is the weaker but honest *cheaply probe both operations per model*." The abstract (lines 50-51: "we withdraw the 'rule indexed by imprinting' and conclude you must probe both per model"), contributions (lines 90-92: "which wins does not reduce to a single trait (r=0.29)... so the honest prescription is probe both per model, not 'prime the cache'"), and conclusion (line 634) all reflect the correction. Related Work was left un-updated, so it over-differentiates from Beyond RAG/SnapKV on a basis the paper no longer supports.

The Related Work sentence also conflates the retained result (§6.2 imprintability predicts banking MAGNITUDE, r=0.94) with the withdrawn operation-choice rule (§7.1, r=0.29). There is no local caveat near line 139 pointing to §7's retraction. This is a genuine (minor) narrative inconsistency, not a false positive.
verdict.revised_fix: Rewrite the last sentence of the "Task-aware and trained caches" paragraph (lines 137-139) to match the corrected body, dropping both "the mode account" as the advertised contribution and "the right task-aware prime is model-specific" as a delivered prescription. Suggested replacement: "Our contribution to this thread is two-fold: (i) a *what-kind* axis on top of prior *how-much* work — imprintability predicts banking *magnitude* (§6.2), and a shuffle control shows the *kind* of content banked is model-specific (token presence for Gemma vs. structure for Mistral; §6.5); and (ii) making task-aware *selection* a first-class, iso-budget baseline against discarded-prefix conditioning (§7). We find that neither conditioning nor selection dominates and that which wins does not reduce to a single trait (r=0.29), so the deployable prescription is to *probe both per model* — while noting the one systematic asymmetry these retention-based methods should heed: aggressive query-aware selection carries a Qwen-family-specific risk that conditioning sidesteps." This keeps the genuine differentiation (what-kind axis; selection-as-baseline; the Qwen selection risk) while removing the withdrawn mode/model-specific-prime prescription.

verdict.reason:
Verified against the paper and the underlying experiments; the issue is real (minor, narrative/evidentiary-consistency).

WHAT I CHECKED:
- The flagged sentence exists at paper_draft_v5.md line 461 (the finding's cited lines 435-438 are ~23 off, but the quoted text and location are unambiguous — the §7 "Relevance (reranking)" paragraph, lines 457-461): "...(for Gemma this is a token-presence re-weighting; §6.5)."
- §6.5 (lines 382-449) establishes token-presence-vs-structure using BANKING probes only: single-fact (exp33) and two-fact binding (exp32). It does NOT test the reranking task.
- The reranking experiment (exp14c, make_numbers.py lines 70-78) has conditions bare/generic/tfidf_plain only — NO shuffle condition. So its numbers (gemma12b/27b +0.036* MRR) confirm the effect exists but say nothing about token-presence vs structure for reranking.
- A reranking-shuffle run genuinely IS in-flight: experiments/13_contrastive/{chain_rerank_shuffle.sh,run_rerank_shuffle_only.sh} define exp34_rerank_shuffle with a tfidf_shuffled condition, and /results/exp34_rerank_shuffle/ exists but is EMPTY (not yet run). This validates the finding's "in progress" framing.

DOUBLE-STANDARD CONFIRMED: For the adjacent QA task (§7, lines 463-475) the paper ran a task-specific shuffle (ordered vs shuffled question prime, explicit "order contribution" row) BEFORE concluding token presence. For reranking it asserts the same mechanism by citing §6.5 (a different task) without a task-specific control. The authors' own behavior on QA shows they consider per-task confirmation necessary, making the reranking assertion inconsistent with the standard they set.

NOT ALREADY CAVEATED: Limitations (lines 590-613) flag reranking's query-likelihood weakness and the single-family token-presence limitation, but never note that the reranking mechanism is untested/hypothesized. So the paper does not handle this.

Weighing the counterargument: §6.5 frames token-presence as a model-level family trait ("predominantly token presence for the Gemma family"), so applying it to reranking is a defensible generalization — this is why the severity is genuinely minor, not major. But the parenthetical's phrasing states it as established for this task ("this IS a token-presence re-weighting; §6.5"), which slightly overstates the evidence relative to the paper's own scrupulous, task-specific standard elsewhere.
verdict.revised_fix:
Soften the parenthetical mechanistic gloss on line 461 from an assertion to a hypothesis pending the reranking shuffle run. Change:

"which is exactly what relevance scoring rewards (for Gemma this is a token-presence re-weighting; §6.5)."

to:

"which is exactly what relevance scoring rewards (we expect this to be a token-presence re-weighting for the Gemma family, per §6.5; a reranking-specific shuffle control (exp34, tfidf_shuffled) is in progress)."

This keeps the model-level generalization from §6.5 (legitimate) while making clear the reranking-task shuffle has not yet been measured — matching the standard the paper applies to QA (which does report an explicit task-specific shuffle). Optionally add a one-line note to §10 Limitations that the reranking mechanism (token-presence vs structure) is inferred from the §6.5 model-level trait and awaits a task-specific shuffle control.
